sentences.text: [TEST PATTERN TONE]
I think there would be many things to tell, but then I just can't think of it.
[CHUCKLES] I just can't think what to say and what happened, and so on.
Because actually [INAUDIBLE] main thing I go to [INAUDIBLE].
We had to move, that we had to move.
And we had to be-- and we were lucky that we had a nice apartment.
And the family was together.
And we hope-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we hoped, you know, that that's the way we'll have to live.
No, no, I-- I never left the ghetto.
No.
No.
Yeah, of course, like-- like my husband, and people like him, who worked for German company.
Like it was my husband's father's company, but the German took over.
So it was-- and he got a paper, and that he's working for a German company, and he could go out.
And it helped a lot.
It helped a lot, because people who could go out, they would buy stuff.
There was not much to-- to buy in the ghetto.
But out of the ghetto there was lots of food, things like that they need, they could when they went out.
They couldn't go far, too, with-- with the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] start of the-- with the stars [?on their back?].
But the Poles would come to-- the Poles would come to the to the ghetto, not inside the ghetto, but towards the doors, and so.
And they would-- they would sell what stuff what was needed in the ghetto.
Of course.
Sure.
There was that-- that was some succeeded and some didn't.
But they tried to get out.
They were shot.
They-- most of them, they had to go back.
But many of them were shot.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There's no monkey business with them.
People came and told us.
They were the Polish people.
They saw what was going on.
And-- and somehow we found out, you know, what happened.
We couldn't believe it, but [INAUDIBLE] time we found out that what was going on there.
How what?
It was some people they had-- they were active.
They had their own [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had their own office.
And there was some people they would go regularly, like one of my brother-in-law there was very active.
And they tried to help the people there.

And-- but it didn't pay to be active, because what-- what they did, one fine day, they ask the-- the office to turn-- to give the names of the 10 most popular, most known people from the ghetto, you know?
And sure enough, one of them was my [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was my brother-in-law.
What was his name?
Morganbesser.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was one of the 10 people.
And they come and picked him up.
And he never came back.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And like there was a-- usually the German give [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] orders.
They give orders to the Jewish community.
And then it was a secret.
But people found out, you know, what we started to-- then they knew there would be something going on.
So this one time, we too, we heard that there will be something going on.
And-- and it was special.
They say, that will be for older-- they will take the older people.
So when I heard that-- heard that.
And I told my mother she shouldn't come home.
My husband had close friends from university out there in-- who lived in Krakow, and whom he trusted.
And we told her she should go there-- there to these people.
And when everything will calm down, then she'll come home.
But she shouldn't come home this time.
So what shall I tell you?
The day passed by.
And who comes in, my mother.
Came home.
Came back.
I was just frantic.
I'll never forget it, how frantic I was, and upset that she came home.
You know, I told her not to come.
And I was yelling at her.
But-- but she was home.
Why was she home?
She worried about me.
She was a Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH].
She worried about me, because I wasn't feeling too well at that time.
I had some kind of a cold of something.
And then-- and sure enough the next day, everybody that lived [INAUDIBLE] to be on the street.
And it was a so-called, [NON-ENGLISH].
It was that they came and they took people.
They-- they took the people as they were outside, and the one to the left, or one to do right.
You never knew.
And [INAUDIBLE] my mother was one of them.
And they took her.
And I never saw her again.
Never forget it.
I wasn't taken, my mother was.
Yeah, yeah.
We're still-- still in the apartment.
We stayed until they were-- until there everybody had to go to Plaszow, when they started with Plaszow-- Krakow-Plaszow there on the hill.
That's when it was my next [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] walk.
We had to walk.
Only what you could carry yourself.
What with some stuff we had hidden, or some stuff we had-- had Polish friends give.
So then we could take it out and-- you know.
But there wasn't much we could take along.
It-- the the climate wasn't bad, but I really couldn't say.
Now I don't remember.
I just don't remember.
And I don't want to tell you stories.
It was a nice walk.
It was a nice walk.
I have another story which I don't want to miss.
But that is the story when we-- when we had to leave Plaszow; when they took us out from Plaszow.
And again, we were standing and waiting to be-- to be picked up.
And there was a young boy who-- whose family I knew.
And we became-- and what-- kind of looked after him because that he was the only one from the family left.
He had the brother lost, and the parents, and the sister.
Anyhow, and we were close, so--
Henry.
His name was Henry.
And he came to say goodbye to me, when you know, they know that they will take us away [INAUDIBLE].
So I said to him, listen.
I had some pictures here from home.
And from that I would like to keep the pictures.
I have them hidden there and there, where the wood was between this.
I have them hidden there and there.
Because we were not allowed to have anything to carry or anything.
But I had it hidden.
And I said, if you can, put them away somewhere.
Because if I survive, I would like to keep them.
So if you can put it away somewhere else and save it.
And he said, so I still hear him say, Else I promise.
I promise you will have the pictures.
What shall I tell you?
The time was passing by.
I survived.
He survived.
I was back home in Bielitz, in my town.
And he was in Krakow.
And one fine day I get-- I got a letter, and it says, Else, if you want to see your pictures, then you have to come to Krakow.
[LAUGHS]
I didn't lose much time.
And in a few days I went to Krakow.
And he-- and he went with me to Plaszow there.
And we went up to Plaszow.
And there was a guard.
We just couldn't go in there.
There was a Russian guard that-- whom we told that we-- we were prisoners there, and that we have some pictures taken.
Could we get them?
And he-- he went for permission to take us.
And that one guard took us there.
And Henry, he brought us to a-- to a water-- where water was.
How do you say that?
A lake?
It wasn't a lake.
Yeah.
Yeah.
And he says Else, here are your pictures.
I say, Henry, [INAUDIBLE] have the pictures in the water.
What are you talking about?
Don't worry, you'll get them.
And it was a hot summer day.
He didn't even know how to swim.
He had to swim that, but he took off his underpants.
And he would get in, and come out with a jar.
He would come out with a jar.
So I say to him, what are you talking?
Where will you-- how will I get the pictures in the jar in the water?
He says, don't worry.
They're all right.
And he was a really handy guy.
What he did, he put these pictures into a jar and he glued them-- he had them glued up.
And-- and then again, twice, you know, and put them in.
And sure enough, he got them out.
And they were dry.
And they were not spoiled.
The pictures we will see, the very pictures that were hidden there.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
[DRINKS] [?
I do ?].
All I remember is that we were scared to death.
We were scared.
We didn't know what was going on.
That's a good question.
I don't remember anymore.
I don't know, actually.
I guess we-- I think I walked with my husband, because I still was with him.
Mind you, the men had-- were separate from the women.
We were separate.
But we could meet, you know, in the evening.
So we would meet, and see each other, and talk to each other.
No.
Nothing.
We were not doing anything.
They just kept us there.
They just kept us there.
Pardon?
Yeah, it was often.
Yeah, it was often they were-- they were counting us.
Different, only that I can't put it all together.
I can't put it together.
What?
Yeah, that was a barrack, and they were bunk beds.
That were bunk beds.
And there were-- we had to sleep two in one bunk bed.
And on the main floor, and then higher up.
Always two together.
And we were very close.
There was-- our husbands were cousins.
And we became very close.
And I slept with [? there ?].
[DRINKS] No bathrooms.
But yeah, so to say bathroom, not like we understand on the bathroom.
They were-- they took us on the showers, they cleaned, because it was-- it was very-- Jew was dirty.
[?knew that?] So they were afraid always that they will get-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] from that, we'll-- that we'll [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] be dirty.
And what you'll get when you are dirty.
How do you call it?
What?
The typhoid, or some kind of a-- a sickness.
So we had to go-- that's the first thing.
When we came to Auschwitz, we had to change our clothes.
We had to go in the shower--
No, no, no.
No.
In Plaszow, no, because there we still lived in an apartment.
So we had a-- we had a bathroom there.
I was jumping now to-- to Plaszow.
In Plaszow, that's [?where we are?].
But there we had still bathrooms.
And so we could clean-- keep clean.
Where?
In Plaszow or in [INAUDIBLE]?
In Plaszow?
Soup.
There was some kind of soup we get.
One soup.
And we got potatoes, cooked potatoes-- not peeled, just cooked.
And they were just strewn-- strewn on our bed.
And that was it.
Not that I remember, really, that we were beaten, also.
They would sooner shot-- shot us than beat us.
[INAUDIBLE] and the special-- the only one that we were so scared of was Goeth, the lagerfuhrer, the guy that's in charge.
He, when he walked out and out and he met a-- a Jew there, he would just shot them, and that's it.
For no reason at all.
Goeth.
Goeth was his name.
G-O-E-T-H, I think.
Goeth.
No, I wouldn't be here.
[LAUGHS] He knew how to shoot.
No.
But there's another story.
Now do you want me to tell this story from Plaszow?
There was another story.
I'll never forget this.
Then everybody that lived there to be out in-- in Plaszow.
And there was a-- and there was a young man that was under the-- how do you call it?
He was to-- to be hung, to be hung up.
To be hanged?
Yeah.
You know.
And everybody had to pass by, walk by, as a warning that that it will happen to whomever if they try to run away.
Because there was a young man who tried to run away and somehow he was caught when he crossed the-- the line or the camp, or whatever.
And I just happened to stand under-- under that-- how do you call where you hang it?
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Yeah.
On there.
And I, personally, I could hear him talk to Goeth, because Goeth was there, too.
And he said, it's not right what you are doing to us.
He says, you are doing-- you are doing it to me today.
But there will be a day when it will come to you, and you will get it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and he was hanged.
And Goeth, you know, they got finally a hold of Goeth, too, but too late.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Everybody was so afraid of him.
Nobody wanted to be near him.
If we heard-- if we heard that, you know, that Goeth is out from his house, you know, that everybody would stay in the cabin.
Nobody would go out.
It-- it was just empty, the camp.
And when they-- when we heard that Goeth is out.
He was-- he was just not a normal person.
Just a second.
There was just a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we get-- get up when we were called, is when we got up.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] [? here?] I want-- I wanted-- I had another story, but now I forgot it.
It wasn't hard, because they were blowing the horn.
So we have to get up.
And that was it.
Actually, nothing.
We had to get out and be counted.
That was the big stuff, that we have to be counted.
And if it didn't come in time, when it doesn't come out right, we had to be counted again.
[LAUGHS] That's all we had to do, to be there, to see, and to be counted.
It depends.
A few hours, sometimes.
It depends what they had to do.
Sometimes it was a few hours.
They had nothing else for us to do.
But there was a-- there was a group of women who were sewing some-- that they needed sewing, for sewing people.
So they took a number of women who would-- so they would make they would make coats-- like for police [INAUDIBLE], coats, or uniforms.
That there was good to work, because they were left alone.
No, I never sew.
On what?
They were-- they were whoever, that girl.
There was one in-- in charge of the sewing.
And he made a large fortune, because he made it for the army.
[INAUDIBLE] And he didn't need to pay the woman there.
They weren't paying.
So he made a fortune.
But he was very good.
He treated them very nicely.
And it was good to work for him, because then again, you got a-- a card.
You know, you could go out.
And so that time, we got, yeah, and I got the card from my mother, you know, that she could-- that she should be able to work there, and they should be able to come in and out.
And we-- we did it.
And we-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I got the card from my mother that she should go out.
And that's why she could go out to our friends there, you know?
And- and that Goeth, it was-- it was good, because-- not Goeth.
The other one-- the one that was in charge of the sewing.
He was a very good man.
And yeah, that's what I want to say.
He wanted-- he said he can't hire anymore, because he has no machines.
He doesn't have so many machines.
If someone has his own machine can come and work, but he doesn't have.
So that's what we did.
We bought a machine, and and so that my mother, for my mother there that she could get in there, and that she could go.

So she used to go [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there every day for sewing, until-- until one day-- until one day she didn't-- she didn't-- and we told her then, when she goes to work, she shouldn't come home.
That's how it was.
Do you know the name of the man who owned the sewing factory?
No, I don't remember.
I knew, but I don't remember.
I'm just thinking I have to ask someone.
But maybe-- do you have anyone that was in Krakow due to interview?
Maybe they will know better than I.
He was a very good man.
What time of year?
I don't remember.
